text: The surgeon carefully reduces the herniated stomach, including the distal body and pylorus, from the mediastinum back into the abdomen. The interior of the large hernia sac is examined.

The surgeon incises the junction of the hernia sac with the right crus of the diaphragm, using blunt dissection to create a plane between the sac and the mediastinum, entering the mediastinum from outside the sac.

The surgeon incises the peritoneum along the margins of the hiatus, attempting to access the avascular plane between the hernia sac and the mediastinum. Blunt dissection is used to separate the sac from the mediastinum, revealing the inside and outside of the sac amidst adhesions due to the long-standing hernia.

The surgeon completes dissection from six o'clock to twelve o'clock on the right side, reaching towards the apex of the hernia sac deep in the mediastinum. The anterior vagus, left vagus, and esophagus become visible as the sac is reduced. The next step involves dissecting posterior to the esophagus to fully mobilize the gastroesophageal junction.

The surgeon ties umbilical tape around the gastroesophageal (GE) junction, securing it in place. The esophagus is visibly positioned within the abdomen, and traction is applied to ensure the GE junction remains stable and does not retract into the thorax.

The surgeon prepares the stomach for fundoplication by dividing the greater omentum from the margins of the greater curvature of the stomach. Short gastric vessels are divided using harmonic or ligature devices. The right vagus nerve, identified as the posterior vagus, is visible lying on the posterior surface of the esophagus near the gastroesophageal junction.
Following fundic mobilization, the surgeon performs the shoe shine maneuver to prevent twisting of the wrap. The hiatal defect is closed using interrupted non-absorbable Ethymond sutures.

The surgeon performs a 360-degree Nissen fundoplication, ensuring the knot is not too tight to prevent muscle atrophy. The fundoplication is described as 'floppy,' allowing a 5mm instrument to pass between the wrap and the esophagus.

The surgeon places two sutures two centimeters apart to create the wrap, ensuring it covers the distal intra-abdominal part of the esophagus with a total thickness of two centimeters. The completed wrap is fixed to the GE junction, and the tension-free lower part of the esophagus is visible.
The wrap is positioned over the lower two centimeters of the esophagus, and the hiatus is repaired using a composite mesh.
